title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: cdf BB bootstrap replications ti*t_i^*, =1…Bi=1\ldots B bias-correction number z0=Φ(∑iBI(ti*<t0)/B)z_0 = \Phi(\sum_i^B (t_i^* < t_0) / B ) t0=f(x)t_0=f(x) original estimate acceleration number aa measures rate change σt0\sigma_{t_0} xx, data changes. first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Package bcaboot aims make construction bootstrap confidence intervals almost automatic. two main functions : bca_nonpar() nonparametric bootstrap bca_par() parametric bootstrap results returned bcaboot objects consistent structure support tidy(), glance(), autoplot() integration tidyverse ecosystem. details Efron Narasimhan (2020) paper. Much theory behind approach can found references Efron (1987), T. DiCiccio Efron (1992), T. J. DiCiccio Efron (1996), Efron Hastie (2016).

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Suppose wish construct bootstrap confidence intervals R2R^2-statistic linear regression. Using diabetes data lars (442 11) example, use function regress y x, matrix 10 predictors, compute R2R^2. Constructing bootstrap confidence intervals involves merely calling bca_nonpar: result prints clean summary:
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {     y <- Xy[, 11]     X <- Xy[, 1:10]     summary(lm(y ~ X) )$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(x = Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun, verbose = FALSE) result ##  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi ##        0.95 0.4221740 0.5613841 0.4429423 0.5701783 ##        0.90 0.4392968 0.5507219 0.4531704 0.5599503 ##        0.80 0.4541906 0.5387434 0.4649627 0.5481579 ##        0.68 0.4647324 0.5298461 0.4742814 0.5388392

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: tidy() method returns tibble one row per (confidence level, method) combination, following broom conventions: glance() method provides one-row summary bootstrap run:
code: tidy(result) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.422     0.561    0.00776     0.00201 ## 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.443     0.570   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.439     0.551    0.00280     0.00173 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.453     0.560   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.454     0.539    0.00262     0.00157 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.465     0.548   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.465     0.530    0.00143     0.00158 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.474     0.539   NA          NA glance(result) ## # A tibble: 1 × 9 ##   method accel      theta sdboot     z0        a sdjack     B boot_mean ##   <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> ## 1 nonpar regression 0.507 0.0325 -0.268 -0.00585 0.0323  2000     0.515

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: ggplot2 available, autoplot() produces publication-ready plot confidence intervals:
code: library(ggplot2) autoplot(result)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: accel argument controls acceleration aa estimated: accel = "regression" (default): uses local regression bootstrap count vectors nearest uniform. Also computes gbca diagnostic. accel = "jackknife": uses classical delete-one (delete-group) jackknife influence values.
code: set.seed(1234) result_jk <- bca_nonpar(x = Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun,                         accel = "jackknife", verbose = FALSE) tidy(result_jk) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.435     0.560    0.00412     0.00155 ## 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.443     0.570   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.443     0.551    0.00497     0.00224 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.453     0.560   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.454     0.540    0.00127     0.00158 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.465     0.548   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.464     0.531    0.00186     0.00177 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.474     0.539   NA          NA

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: logistic regression fit data 812 neonates large clinic. summary dataset. goal predict death versus survival—yy 1 0, respectively—basis 11 baseline variables one resp particular concern. (207 deaths 605 survivors.) θ\theta, parameter interest coefficient resp. Discussions investigator suggested weighting 4 1 deaths versus non-deaths.

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Parametric bootstrapping context requires us independently sample response according estimated probabilities regression model. discussed paper accompanying software, routine bca_par also requires sufficient statistics β̂=M′y\hat{\beta} = M^\prime y MM model matrix. Therefore, makes sense function work. function glm_boot returns list estimate θ̂\hat{\theta}, bootstrap estimates, sufficient statistics. Now can compute bootstrap estimates using bca_par. can examine results using tidy methods: bootstrap standard error using B=2000B=2000 samples resp can read glance output:
code: weights <- with(neonates, ifelse(y == 0, 1, 4)) glm_model <- glm(formula = y ~ ., family = "binomial", weights = weights, data = neonates) summary(glm_model) ##  ## Call: ## glm(formula = y ~ ., family = "binomial", data = neonates, weights = weights) ##  ## Coefficients: ##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     ## (Intercept) -0.26510    0.07598  -3.489 0.000485 *** ## gest        -0.70602    0.13117  -5.383 7.35e-08 *** ## ap          -0.78594    0.07874  -9.982  < 2e-16 *** ## bwei        -0.23332    0.12592  -1.853 0.063879 .   ## gen         -0.04107    0.07355  -0.558 0.576594     ## resp         0.94306    0.08974  10.509  < 2e-16 *** ## head         0.04813    0.08057   0.597 0.550299     ## hr           0.03504    0.07191   0.487 0.626045     ## cpap         0.43438    0.08869   4.898 9.70e-07 *** ## age          0.15727    0.08492   1.852 0.064041 .   ## temp        -0.05960    0.08506  -0.701 0.483520     ## size        -0.37477    0.09919  -3.778 0.000158 *** ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) ##  ##     Null deviance: 1951.7  on 811  degrees of freedom ## Residual deviance: 1187.2  on 800  degrees of freedom ## AIC: 1211.2 ##  ## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5 glm_boot <- function(B, glm_model, weights, var = "resp") {     pi_hat <- glm_model$fitted.values     n <- length(pi_hat)     y_star <- sapply(seq_len(B), function(i) ifelse(runif(n) <= pi_hat, 1, 0))     beta_star <- apply(y_star, 2, function(y) {         boot_data <- glm_model$data         boot_data$y <- y         coef(glm(formula = y ~ ., data = boot_data, weights = weights, family = "binomial"))     })     list(theta = coef(glm_model)[var],          theta_star = beta_star[var, ],          suff_stat = t(y_star) %*% model.matrix(glm_model)) } set.seed(3891) glm_boot_out <- glm_boot(B = 2000, glm_model = glm_model, weights = weights) glm_bca <- bca_par(t0 = glm_boot_out$theta,                    tt = glm_boot_out$theta_star,                    bb = glm_boot_out$suff_stat) glm_bca ##  conf.level    bca.lo   bca.hi    std.lo   std.hi ##        0.95 0.5981733 1.213676 0.6394385 1.246691 ##        0.90 0.6549470 1.167789 0.6882535 1.197876 ##        0.80 0.7169529 1.113660 0.7445341 1.141595 ##        0.68 0.7635391 1.067160 0.7890090 1.097120 tidy(glm_bca) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.943    0.598      1.21    0.0247      0.0125  ## 2       0.95 standard    0.943    0.639      1.25   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.943    0.655      1.17    0.0193      0.00848 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.943    0.688      1.20   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.943    0.717      1.11    0.0107      0.00907 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.943    0.745      1.14   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.943    0.764      1.07    0.00676     0.00507 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.943    0.789      1.10   NA          NA glance(glm_bca) ## # A tibble: 1 × 9 ##   method accel theta sdboot     z0       a sdjack     B boot_mean ##   <chr>  <chr> <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> ## 1 par    NA    0.943  0.155 -0.215 -0.0194     NA  2000     0.982

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Now suppose wish use nonstandard estimation procedure, example, via glmnet package, uses cross-validation figure best fit, corresponding penalization parameter λ\lambda (named lambda.min). can examine estimates lambda.min follows. Following lines , create helper function perform bootstrap. go. can compare models side--side:
code: X <- as.matrix(neonates[, seq_len(11)]) ; Y <- neonates$y; glmnet_model <- glmnet::cv.glmnet(x = X, y = Y, family = "binomial", weights = weights) coefs <- as.matrix(coef(glmnet_model, s = glmnet_model$lambda.min)) knitr::kable(data.frame(variable = rownames(coefs), coefficient = coefs[, 1]), row.names = FALSE, digits = 3) glmnet_boot <- function(B, X, y, glmnet_model, weights, var = "resp") {     lambda <- glmnet_model$lambda.min     theta <- as.matrix(coef(glmnet_model, s = lambda))     pi_hat <- predict(glmnet_model, newx = X, s = "lambda.min", type = "response")     n <- length(pi_hat)     y_star <- sapply(seq_len(B), function(i) ifelse(runif(n) <= pi_hat, 1, 0))     beta_star <- apply(y_star, 2,                        function(y) {                            as.matrix(coef(glmnet::glmnet(x = X, y = y, lambda = lambda, weights = weights, family = "binomial")))                        })      rownames(beta_star) <- rownames(theta)     list(theta = theta[var, ],          theta_star = beta_star[var, ],          suff_stat = t(y_star) %*% X) } glmnet_boot_out <- glmnet_boot(B = 2000, X, y, glmnet_model, weights) glmnet_bca <- bca_par(t0 = glmnet_boot_out$theta,                       tt = glmnet_boot_out$theta_star,                       bb = glmnet_boot_out$suff_stat) autoplot(glm_bca) + ggtitle("glm model") autoplot(glmnet_bca) + ggtitle("glmnet model")

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Assume two independent estimates variance normal theory: σ̂12∼σ12χn12n1, \hat{\sigma}_1^2\sim\frac{\sigma_1^2\chi_{n_1}^2}{n_1}, σ̂22∼σ22χn22n2. \hat{\sigma}_2^2\sim\frac{\sigma_2^2\chi_{n_2}^2}{n_2}. Suppose now parameter interest θ=σ12σ22     \theta=\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} wish compute confidence limits. setting, theory yields exact limits: θ̂(α)=θ̂Fn1,n21−α. \hat{\theta}(\alpha) = \frac{\hat{\theta}}{F_{n_1,n_2}^{1-\alpha}}. can apply bca_par problem. , helper functions. Note additional function funcF corresponds τ(β̂*)\tau(\hat{\beta}^*) paper. function expressing parameter interest function sample. tidy output shows BCa, standard, ABC limits: can compare exact F-distribution limits: Clearly BCa limits match exact values well suggests large upward correction standard limits.
code: ratio_boot <- function(B, v1, v2) {     s1 <- sqrt(v1) * rchisq(n = B, df = n1)  / n1     s2 <- sqrt(v2) * rchisq(n = B, df = n2)  / n2     theta_star <- s1 / s2     beta_star <- cbind(s1, s2)     list(theta = v1 / v2,          theta_star = theta_star,          suff_stat = beta_star) }  funcF <- function(beta) {     beta[1] / beta[2] } B <- 16000; n1 <- 10; n2 <- 42 ratio_boot_out <- ratio_boot(B, 1, 1) ratio_bca <- bca_par(t0 = ratio_boot_out$theta,                      tt = ratio_boot_out$theta_star,                      bb = ratio_boot_out$suff_stat, func = funcF) tidy(ratio_bca) ## # A tibble: 12 × 7 ##    conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##         <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ##  1       0.95 abc             1   0.405       3.42   NA           NA      ##  2       0.95 bca             1   0.412       3.14    0.00824      0.0957 ##  3       0.95 standard        1  -0.0527      2.05   NA           NA      ##  4       0.9  abc             1   0.472       2.73   NA           NA      ##  5       0.9  bca             1   0.475       2.57    0.00743      0.0426 ##  6       0.9  standard        1   0.117       1.88   NA           NA      ##  7       0.8  abc             1   0.562       2.15   NA           NA      ##  8       0.8  bca             1   0.559       2.09    0.00687      0.0316 ##  9       0.8  standard        1   0.312       1.69   NA           NA      ## 10       0.68 abc             1   0.646       1.81   NA           NA      ## 11       0.68 bca             1   0.639       1.77    0.00635      0.0247 ## 12       0.68 standard        1   0.466       1.53   NA           NA exact <- 1 / qf(df1 = n1, df2 = n2, p = 1 - as.numeric(rownames(ratio_bca$limits))) knitr::kable(cbind(ratio_bca$limits, exact = exact), digits = 3) autoplot(ratio_bca)

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Version 1.0 introduces new API. old functions continue work emit deprecation warnings. migrate:

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: new objects use $limits ($lims), $B_mean ($B.mean), $stats always 2-row matrix (never named vector). Use tidy() glance() instead accessing list components directly:
code: ## Old way result$lims[, "bca"] result$stats["theta"]  ## New way tidy(result)    # tibble of confidence limits glance(result)  # tibble of summary statistics

title: Authors and Citation
text: Bradley Efron. Author. Balasubramanian Narasimhan. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Efron B, Narasimhan B (2020). “Automatic Construction Bootstrap Confidence Intervals.” Journal Computational Graphical Statistics, 29(3), 608–619. doi:10.1080/10618600.2020.1714633.
code: @Article{,   title = {The Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals},   author = {Bradley Efron and Balasubramanian Narasimhan},   journal = {Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics},   year = {2020},   volume = {29},   number = {3},   pages = {608--619},   doi = {10.1080/10618600.2020.1714633},   publisher = {Taylor & Francis}, }

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
text: Bias-corrected accelerated (BCa) bootstrap confidence intervals, computed almost automatically. package provides two main functions: bca_nonpar() nonparametric bootstrap bca_par() parametric bootstrap Results integrate tidyverse via tidy(), glance(), autoplot() methods.

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
code: # From CRAN install.packages("bcaboot")  # Development version # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("bnaras/bcaboot")

title: Bias Corrected Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
code: library(bcaboot)  data(diabetes) Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {     y <- Xy[, 11]; X <- Xy[, 1:10]     summary(lm(y ~ X))$adj.r.squared }  set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(Xy, B = 2000, func = rfun, verbose = FALSE) result ##  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi ##        0.95 0.4221740 0.5613841 0.4429423 0.5701783 ##        0.90 0.4392968 0.5507219 0.4531704 0.5599503 ##        0.80 0.4541906 0.5387434 0.4649627 0.5481579 ##        0.68 0.4647324 0.5298461 0.4742814 0.5388392 tidy(result) ## # A tibble: 8 × 7 ##   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high ##        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> ## 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.422     0.561    0.00776     0.00201 ## 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.443     0.570   NA          NA       ## 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.439     0.551    0.00280     0.00173 ## 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.453     0.560   NA          NA       ## 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.454     0.539    0.00262     0.00157 ## 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.465     0.548   NA          NA       ## 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.465     0.530    0.00143     0.00158 ## 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.474     0.539   NA          NA library(ggplot2) autoplot(result)

what: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
title: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
text: Computes nonparametric bias-corrected accelerated (BCa) bootstrap confidence intervals. Consolidates functionality deprecated bcajack, bcajack2, bcanon functions.

title: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
code: bca_nonpar(   x,   B,   func,   ...,   accel = c("regression", "jackknife"),   conf.level = c(0.95, 0.9, 0.8, 0.68),   n_groups = nrow(x),   group_reps = 5,   kl_fraction = 0.333,   n_jack = 2,   jack_groups = 12,   boot_data = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
text: x \(n \times p\) data matrix. Rows observed \(p\)-vectors, assumed independently sampled. \(p = 1\) x can vector. B Number bootstrap replications (integer). func Function computing parameter interest. func(x, ...) return scalar data matrix. ... Additional arguments passed func. accel Method estimating acceleration: "regression" (default) uses local regression bootstrap count vectors nearest uniform; "jackknife" uses classical delete-one (delete-group) jackknife influence values. conf.level Confidence levels intervals (e.g., c(0.95, 0.90, 0.80, 0.68)). values (0, 1) used. n_groups Number groups jackknife. Default nrow(x) gives delete-one jackknife. Set smaller (e.g., 20-40) speed computation large datasets. group_reps n_groups < nrow(x), number random grouping repetitions average . used accel = "jackknife". kl_fraction Fraction bootstrap count vectors nearest uniform used local regression. used accel = "regression". Default 1/3. n_jack Number delete-d jackknife repetitions estimating internal (Monte Carlo) standard error. Set 0 skip. jack_groups Number groups per jackknife fold. boot_data Optional pre-computed bootstrap data: list components Y (B x n count matrix), tt (bootstrap replicates), t0 (original estimate). provided, B ignored resampling performed. verbose Logical; show progress bar bootstrap.

title: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
text: object class "bcaboot" components: limits 9-row matrix confidence limits (columns: bca, jacksd, std, pct) stats 2-row matrix estimates jackknife SEs (columns: theta, sdboot, z0, , sdjack) B_mean Bootstrap sample size mean replicates ustats Bias-corrected estimator SE diagnostic gbca diagnostic (accel = "regression")

title: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
text: Efron B (1987). Better bootstrap confidence intervals. JASA 82, 171-200. Efron B Narasimhan B (2020). Automatic Construction Bootstrap Confidence Intervals. Journal Computational Graphical Statistics, 29(3), 608-619. doi:10.1080/10618600.2020.1714633

title: Nonparametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_nonpar
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {   y <- Xy[, 11]   X <- Xy[, 1:10]   summary(lm(y ~ X))$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(x = Xy, B = 1000, func = rfun, n_groups = 34,                      verbose = FALSE) print(result) #>  #> ── BCa Bootstrap Confidence Intervals  #> Method: nonpar (regression acceleration) #> B = 1000, theta = 0.5065603, sdboot = 0.02988642 #>  #> Confidence limits: #>  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi #>        0.95 0.4205204 0.5544006 0.4479840 0.5651366 #>        0.90 0.4473162 0.5458156 0.4574015 0.5557191 #>        0.80 0.4574096 0.5349201 0.4682593 0.5448613 #>        0.68 0.4654674 0.5272497 0.4768395 0.5362811 #>  #> Diagnostics: #> z0 = -0.3318533, a = -0.01267648, sdjack = 0.03047356

what: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
title: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
text: Computes parametric bootstrap confidence intervals real-valued parameter theta p-parameter exponential family.

title: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
code: bca_par(   t0,   tt,   bb,   conf.level = c(0.95, 0.9, 0.8, 0.68),   n_jack = 6,   jack_groups = 10,   truncation = 0.001,   kl_fraction = 0.333,   conf_density = FALSE,   func )

title: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
text: t0 Observed estimate theta, usually maximum likelihood. tt vector B parametric bootstrap replications theta. bb B p matrix natural sufficient vectors, p dimension exponential family. conf.level Confidence levels intervals. n_jack Number jackknife repetitions internal SE. Set 0 skip. jack_groups Number groups per jackknife fold. truncation Truncation parameter acceleration calculation. Can vector compute acceleration multiple levels. kl_fraction Proportion "nearby" b vectors used gradient estimation. conf_density Logical; TRUE, return BCa confidence density weights. func Optional function ABC (analytical bootstrap) limits.

title: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
text: object class "bcaboot" components: limits 9-row matrix confidence limits stats 2-row matrix estimates jackknife SEs B_mean Bootstrap sample size mean replicates ustats Bias-corrected estimator SE abc ABC limits stats (func provided) conf_density Confidence density weights (requested) accel_matrix Acceleration multiple truncation levels

title: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
text: Efron B (1987). Better bootstrap confidence intervals. JASA 82, 171-200. DiCiccio T Efron B (1992). accurate confidence intervals exponential families. Biometrika, 231-245. Efron B Narasimhan B (2020). Automatic Construction Bootstrap Confidence Intervals. Journal Computational Graphical Statistics, 29(3), 608-619. doi:10.1080/10618600.2020.1714633

title: Parametric BCa bootstrap confidence intervals — bca_par
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") X <- diabetes$x y <- scale(diabetes$y, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE) lm.model <- lm(y ~ X - 1) mu.hat <- lm.model$fitted.values sigma.hat <- stats::sd(lm.model$residuals) t0 <- summary(lm.model)$adj.r.squared y.star <- sapply(mu.hat, rnorm, n = 500, sd = sigma.hat) tt <- apply(y.star, 1, function(y) summary(lm(y ~ X - 1))$adj.r.squared) b.star <- y.star %*% X set.seed(1234) bca_par(t0 = t0, tt = tt, bb = b.star) #>  #> ── BCa Bootstrap Confidence Intervals  #> Method: par #> B = 500, theta = 0.5065862, sdboot = 0.0297771 #>  #> Confidence limits: #>  conf.level    bca.lo    bca.hi    std.lo    std.hi #>        0.95 0.4472066 0.5565305 0.4482242 0.5649483 #>        0.90 0.4518055 0.5478429 0.4576072 0.5555652 #>        0.80 0.4626100 0.5349231 0.4684253 0.5447471 #>        0.68 0.4706893 0.5267426 0.4769741 0.5361983 #>  #> Diagnostics: #> z0 = -0.3107377, a = 0.01129753

what: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — bcaboot-package
title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — bcaboot-package
text: Bootstrap confidence intervals depend three elements: () cumulative distribution bootstrap replications, (b) bias-correction, (c) acceleration number measures rate change standard deviation estimate data changes.  first two depend bootstrap distribution, generated: parametrically non-parametrically. Therefore, difference parametric bca analysis lie nonparametric estimation acceleration, often negligible error.

title: Automatic Construction of Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — bcaboot-package
text: Maintainer: Balasubramanian Narasimhan naras@stat.stanford.edu Authors: Bradley Efron brad@stat.stanford.edu

title: Nonparametric BCa (deprecated) — bcajack
text: bcajack() deprecated bcaboot 1.0 favour bca_nonpar().

title: Nonparametric BCa (deprecated) — bcajack
code: bcajack(   x,   B,   func,   ...,   m = nrow(x),   mr = 5,   K = 2,   J = 10,   alpha = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.16),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Nonparametric BCa (deprecated) — bcajack
text: x \(n \times p\) data matrix. Rows observed \(p\)-vectors, assumed independently sampled. \(p = 1\) x can vector. B Number bootstrap replications (integer). func Function computing parameter interest. func(x, ...) return scalar data matrix. ... Additional arguments passed func. m Number groups (now n_groups). mr Group repetitions (now group_reps). K Jackknife repetitions (now n_jack). J Jackknife groups (now jack_groups). alpha Alpha levels (now use conf.level). verbose Logical; show progress bar bootstrap.

title: Nonparametric BCa via regression (deprecated) — bcajack2
text: x \(n \times p\) data matrix. Rows observed \(p\)-vectors, assumed independently sampled. \(p = 1\) x can vector. B Number bootstrap replications (integer). func Function computing parameter interest. func(x, ...) return scalar data matrix. ... Additional arguments passed func. m Number groups (now n_groups). mr Unused (included compatibility). pct KL fraction (now kl_fraction). K Jackknife repetitions (now n_jack). J Jackknife groups (now jack_groups). alpha Alpha levels (now use conf.level). verbose Logical; show progress bar bootstrap.

title: Parametric BCa (deprecated) — bcapar
text: bcapar() deprecated bcaboot 1.0 favour bca_par().

title: Parametric BCa (deprecated) — bcapar
Parametric BCa (deprecated) — bcapar
text: t0 Observed estimate theta, usually maximum likelihood. tt vector B parametric bootstrap replications theta. bb B p matrix natural sufficient vectors, p dimension exponential family. alpha Alpha levels (now use conf.level). J Jackknife groups (now jack_groups). K Jackknife repetitions (now n_jack). trun Truncation (now truncation). pct KL fraction (now kl_fraction). cd Confidence density flag (now conf_density). func Optional function ABC (analytical bootstrap) limits.

what: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
text: BCa percentile formula: maps nominal alpha levels bias--acceleration adjusted percentiles bootstrap distribution. Efron (1987) Sec 2.

title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
code: compute_bca_limits(z0, a, zalpha, boot_reps)

title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
text: z0 Bias-correction constant (scalar). Acceleration constant (scalar). zalpha Normal quantiles desired alpha levels (vector). boot_reps Sorted vector B bootstrap replicates.

title: Compute BCa confidence limits from bootstrap replicates. — compute_bca_limits
text: Named list limits (BCa quantiles), bca_idx (indices sorted boot_reps), iles (adjusted percentile levels).

what: Compute Poisson/multinomial deviance from uniform for bootstrap count matrix. — compute_kl_distance
title: Compute Poisson/multinomial deviance from uniform for bootstrap count matrix. — compute_kl_distance
text: row Y bootstrap count vector. deviance measures far bootstrap sample departs simple random sampling (uniform counts). Limit Yi=0: term equals 2. EN20 appendix.

title: Compute Poisson/multinomial deviance from uniform for bootstrap count matrix. — compute_kl_distance
code: compute_kl_distance(Y)

title: Compute Poisson/multinomial deviance from uniform for bootstrap count matrix. — compute_kl_distance
text: Y B x m count matrix (rows = bootstrap samples, cols = observation counts).

title: Compute Poisson/multinomial deviance from uniform for bootstrap count matrix. — compute_kl_distance
text: Numeric vector length B deviance values.

what: Glance at a bcaboot result — glance.bcaboot
title: Glance at a bcaboot result — glance.bcaboot
text: Returns one-row tibble summarizing bootstrap run.

title: Glance at a bcaboot result — glance.bcaboot
code: # S3 method for class 'bcaboot' glance(x, ...)

title: Glance at a bcaboot result — glance.bcaboot
text: x bcaboot object bca_nonpar() bca_par(). ... Ignored.

title: Glance at a bcaboot result — glance.bcaboot
text: tibble columns: method "nonpar" "par" accel Acceleration method ("regression", "jackknife", NA) theta Point estimate sdboot Bootstrap standard error z0 Bias-correction constant Acceleration constant sdjack Jackknife standard error (available) B Number bootstrap replicates boot_mean Mean bootstrap replicates

title: Glance at a bcaboot result — glance.bcaboot
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {   y <- Xy[, 11]; X <- Xy[, 1:10]   summary(lm(y ~ X))$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(Xy, 1000, rfun, n_groups = 34, verbose = FALSE) glance(result) #> # A tibble: 1 × 9 #>   method accel      theta sdboot     z0       a sdjack     B boot_mean #>   <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 nonpar regression 0.507 0.0299 -0.332 -0.0127 0.0305  1000     0.517

text: Returns tibble one row per (confidence level, method) combination, following broom conventions.

title: Tidy a bcaboot result — tidy.bcaboot
code: # S3 method for class 'bcaboot' tidy(x, ...)

title: Tidy a bcaboot result — tidy.bcaboot
text: x bcaboot object bca_nonpar() bca_par(). ... Ignored.

title: Tidy a bcaboot result — tidy.bcaboot
text: tibble columns: conf.level Confidence level (e.g. 0.95) method "bca" "standard" ("abc" available) estimate Point estimate (theta) conf.low Lower confidence limit conf.high Upper confidence limit jacksd.low Jackknife SE lower limit (BCa ; NA otherwise) jacksd.high Jackknife SE upper limit (BCa ; NA otherwise)

title: Tidy a bcaboot result — tidy.bcaboot
code: data(diabetes, package = "bcaboot") Xy <- cbind(diabetes$x, diabetes$y) rfun <- function(Xy) {   y <- Xy[, 11]; X <- Xy[, 1:10]   summary(lm(y ~ X))$adj.r.squared } set.seed(1234) result <- bca_nonpar(Xy, 1000, rfun, n_groups = 34, verbose = FALSE) tidy(result) #> # A tibble: 8 × 7 #>   conf.level method   estimate conf.low conf.high jacksd.low jacksd.high #>        <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1       0.95 bca         0.507    0.421     0.554    0.0106      0.00304 #> 2       0.95 standard    0.507    0.448     0.565   NA          NA       #> 3       0.9  bca         0.507    0.447     0.546    0.00187     0.00176 #> 4       0.9  standard    0.507    0.457     0.556   NA          NA       #> 5       0.8  bca         0.507    0.457     0.535    0.00284     0.00195 #> 6       0.8  standard    0.507    0.468     0.545   NA          NA       #> 7       0.68 bca         0.507    0.465     0.527    0.00276     0.00180 #> 8       0.68 standard    0.507    0.477     0.536   NA          NA

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: New primary API: bca_nonpar() replaces bcajack(), bcajack2(), bcanon(). bca_par() replaces bcapar(). computation functions now return canonical "bcaboot" object consistent structure: $limits (9x4 matrix), $stats (2x5 matrix), $B_mean, $ustats. old field names ($lims, $B.mean) longer used. User-facing parameter conf.level (e.g., c(0.95, 0.90)) replaces alpha (e.g., c(0.025, 0.05)). Internally, alpha expansion unchanged. Descriptive parameter names: n_groups (m), group_reps (mr), kl_fraction (pct), n_jack (K), jack_groups (J), truncation (trun), conf_density (cd), boot_data (overloaded B). bcanon() removed (never CRAN).

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: bcajack(), bcajack2(), bcapar(), bcaplot() deprecated lifecycle warnings (per session). continue work thin wrappers translate new API.

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: Issue #2: Fixed grouped jackknife (n_groups < n) using sapply(seq_len_m, sample.int, ...) accidentally passed iteration values first positional arg, overriding n= keyword enabling replacement. Replaced matrix(sample.int(n, n-r), nrow=m) correct without-replacement partition. (Reported admash) Issue #7: Fixed dimension drop x single-column matrix (vector input). x[-, ] n x 1 matrix returned vector instead matrix, causing type inconsistency jackknife bootstrap code paths. Added drop = FALSE matrix subsetting passed func. (Reported R180) Issue #4 / #1: regression_accel now checks underdetermined regression (nearby samples < ncol(Y)) errors actionable message instead silently returning NAs. Suggests increasing B, increasing kl_fraction, switching accel = "jackknife". (Reported Tim Pollington, Thomas Covert) PR #8: Fixed missing return() K = 0 early-exit path bcajack(), bcajack2(), bcapar(), caused execution fall K > 0 code. rewrite, early exits use explicit return(new_bcaboot(...)). (Reported Bettina Gruen)

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: tidy() method: returns tibble one row per (confidence level, method) pair, following broom conventions (conf.level, method, estimate, conf.low, conf.high). glance() method: returns one-row tibble summarizing bootstrap run (method, accel, theta, sdboot, z0, , sdjack, B, boot_mean). autoplot() method: ggplot2 visualization BCa vs standard confidence intervals. Requires ggplot2 (Suggests). Improved print(): formatted summary confidence limits table instead raw list dump. bca_nonpar() always computes gbca diagnostic using accel = "regression" (tryCatch robustness). user-facing messages use cli consistent formatting.

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: Added cli, tibble, generics, lifecycle Imports. Added ggplot2 Suggests (autoplot()).

title: bcaboot 1.0
text: Shared helpers extracted R/bca_core.R: expand_alpha(), bootstrap_resample(), jackknife_accel(), regression_accel(), compute_ustats(). new_bcaboot() canonical constructor guarantees consistent return structure across computation functions. Test suite expanded 14 34 tests, including adapter tests proving numerical equivalence pre-1.0 fixtures.
